meta.name: Combative Head Injury — Chemical Sedation and RSI

patient.name: Jordan Reeves
patient.chiefComplaint: Combative and hypoxic after motorcycle vs. guardrail MVC with helmet ejection

history.hpi: 38-year-old male involved in a single-vehicle motorcycle crash on a highway ramp. Lost control of the bike and struck a guardrail at approximately 50 mph. Helmet came off during the slide. Found combative, swinging at fire crew on arrival. Unable to give history.
history.events: Single-vehicle motorcycle MVC with helmet ejection. Obvious head injury and scalp laceration. Became progressively more agitated and combative after fire crew made contact.

scene.dispatch: Medic 2, respond code 3 to I-94 eastbound, milepost 297, for a motorcycle MVC with guardrail. 38-year-old male, combative, fire on scene.
scene.location: Highway ramp shoulder. Motorcycle on its side approximately 30 feet from the guardrail impact point. Patient sitting on the grass beyond the guardrail, fire crew attempting to control him.
scene.safety: Lane partially closed by law enforcement. Patient is combative — ensure multiple providers and police are in position before approach. Broken motorcycle parts, leaking fuel.
scene.bystanders: Fire crew on scene; bystander called 911 and witnessed the crash; state trooper present
visualCues: Adult male sitting upright in the grass, swinging his arms at responders
Large scalp laceration above the left ear with active bright red bleeding
Helmet lying 20 feet away on the road
Obvious facial trauma with blood around the mouth and nose
Patient is loudly moaning and yelling incoherently
Not following any commands; any approach results in swinging arms
Motorcycle on its side, leather jacket partially shredded

phases.name: Arrival — Combative Head Injury
phases.description: Crew arrives to find patient agitated to the point where any assessment, oxygenation, or bleeding control is impossible. SpO2 reads 84% when briefly captured. Clearly head-injured with facial trauma and blood in the airway. Agitation is presumed to be from hypoxia and head injury (per MATC Behavioral protocol exclusion criteria, medical/traumatic causes must be treated medically, not behaviorally). Crew must use chemical sedation — specifically Ketamine — to gain control so they can actually treat him.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Blood in mouth, impossible to suction or place OPA due to combativeness
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Rapid, shallow; accessory muscle use intermittently visible
clinicalPresentation.pupils: Cannot reliably assess due to agitation; appear unequal on brief glimpse
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Swinging extremities, purposeful but uncoordinated, resisting all contact
otherFindings: Large left parietal scalp laceration with pulsatile bleeding
Facial trauma with possible midface instability
Agitation presumed due to hypoxia and TBI — NOT primary behavioral
Unable to obtain accurate vitals in this state
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Yelling incoherent profanity, not following commands

expectedActions.action: Ensure scene safety with law enforcement and fire crew assistance for physical containment during drug administration
Attempt manual cervical stabilization as feasible — not possible until patient is sedated
Recognize that this agitation is from hypoxia + head injury, NOT a primary behavioral emergency — treat per Head Injury protocol
expectedActions.rationale: The Agitated/Violent Behavioral protocol explicitly excludes head injury and metabolic causes. This is medical agitation requiring medical treatment.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.action: Administer Ketamine 4 mg/kg IM (360 mg for 90 kg patient) in the lateral thigh to achieve dissociative sedation; 3-5 minute onset
expectedActions.rationale: IM ketamine is the fastest way to gain chemical control when IV access is impossible. Dissociative dose will allow assessment and airway management while preserving respiratory drive and hemodynamics.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.action: Apply direct pressure to scalp laceration (use the moment of sedation onset) — avoid NPA due to facial trauma
Prepare RSI equipment and team during the 3-5 minute onset window — this is a bridge, not a destination

phases.name: Dissociated — Rapid Assessment and RSI Preparation
phases.description: IM ketamine has taken effect. The patient is dissociated — eyes open, not following commands, no purposeful movement. Respiratory drive preserved. Crew can now perform a meaningful assessment, apply high-flow oxygen, control bleeding, and establish IV access while preparing for definitive airway.
phases.triggerCondition: Dissociation achieved approximately 3-5 minutes after IM ketamine

transitions.condition: Successful RSI with confirmed tube placement

clinicalPresentation.airway: Now able to suction blood and secretions; jaw relaxed; no gag
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Spontaneous, adequate rate and depth with ketamine (respiratory drive preserved)
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Tachycardic, BP slightly elevated from ketamine sympathomimetic effect
clinicalPresentation.pupils: LEFT pupil 6 mm sluggishly reactive, RIGHT 4 mm reactive — now assessable; concerning for herniation
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Dissociated — eyes open, not following, no purposeful movement
otherFindings: Now possible to assess: GCS pre-dissociation was likely ~11-12, with motor component resisting
Unequal pupils with left dilation concerning for ipsilateral mass effect
Scalp hemorrhage controlled with direct pressure

expectedActions.action: Apply cervical collar and in-line stabilization now possible
Apply high-flow oxygen via NRB; target SpO2 ≥94%
Suction blood and secretions from oropharynx
Maintain direct pressure on scalp laceration; consider pressure dressing; no tourniquet for scalp
Establish two large-bore IVs; draw blood for destination
Re-assess neurologic status: GCS, pupils, motor — document pre-intubation baseline
Maintain SBP ≥110 mmHg per TBI protocol
Preoxygenate with BVM + PEEP 5 cmH2O and apneic oxygenation NC 15 LPM
Administer Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV push (90 mg for 90 kg patient) — additional induction not needed since ketamine is already on board for dissociation
expectedActions.rationale: The 360 mg IM ketamine provides more than enough induction. Paralytic alone is what's needed to convert the dissociated state into intubating conditions.

expectedActions.action: Perform video laryngoscopy intubation with in-line cervical stabilization; 8.0 mm cuffed ETT; use SALAD for blood in airway
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds, absent epigastric sounds

phases.name: Post-Intubation Neuroprotective Management
phases.description: Tube confirmed. Crew manages per Head Injury protocol: maintain normocapnia, SBP ≥110, head of bed elevated, and expedited trauma center transport. Post-intubation sedation with additional ketamine for maintenance.
phases.triggerCondition: Successful RSI with confirmed tube placement

clinicalPresentation.airway: 8.0 mm ETT at 23 cm, cuff inflated, secured
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Ventilated with PEEP 5, symmetric chest rise
clinicalPresentation.pupils: LEFT 6 mm sluggish, RIGHT 4 mm sluggish (post-paralytic reference)

expectedActions.action: Ventilate at 10 breaths/min, 6-8 mL/kg IBW, target EtCO2 35-45 mmHg (aim 40); do NOT hyperventilate despite unequal pupils
Post-intubation sedation: Ketamine 0.5-1 mg/kg IV (45-90 mg) for maintenance + Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg (90-180 mcg) IV for analgesia
Maintain SBP ≥110 mmHg with NS bolus as needed
Elevate head of spine board 30 degrees (reverse Trendelenburg)
Apply pressure dressing to scalp laceration
Expedited transport to Level I trauma center; early trauma team activation
Serial neuro reassessment (pupils) every 5 minutes

phases.name: Branch: Failure to Recognize Medical Agitation
phases.description: Crew treats agitation as a behavioral emergency and attempts physical restraint without chemical sedation, or uses a dose of haloperidol. Without addressing hypoxia, the patient continues to deteriorate. This branch teaches that agitation in trauma is a medical sign, not a behavior.
phases.triggerCondition: Attempted physical restraint without chemical sedation OR use of antipsychotic without addressing hypoxia

clinicalPresentation.pupils: LEFT 7 mm fixed, RIGHT 4 mm sluggish — progression

expectedActions.action: Immediately escalate to ketamine-facilitated RSI; treat as severe TBI with herniation signs
Administer Ketamine 2 mg/kg IV (180 mg) + Rocuronium 1 mg/kg (90 mg) and intubate immediately

criticalActions: Recognize that agitation in a trauma patient is a medical sign (hypoxia, TBI) — NOT behavioral
Coordinate scene safety with law enforcement for drug administration
Administer Ketamine 4 mg/kg IM (360 mg) for dissociation and rapid control
Use the dissociation window to apply C-collar, suction, apply O2, establish IVs, control bleeding
Reassess neuro status (GCS, pupils, motor) once dissociated
Preoxygenate with BVM + PEEP and apneic oxygenation
Administer Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV (90 mg) to convert dissociation to intubating conditions
Perform video laryngoscopy intubation with in-line stabilization and SALAD for blood
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography
Transport to Level I trauma center
expectedActions: Post-intubation sedation with ketamine maintenance + fentanyl
bonusActions: Articulate that IM ketamine provides sedation plus induction for RSI in one step
Avoid additional induction agent on top of IM ketamine — paralytic alone is sufficient
Avoid NPA due to facial trauma and possible basilar fracture

learningObjectives: Distinguish medical/traumatic agitation from primary behavioral emergencies
Apply IM ketamine as a bridge to definitive airway management in the combative trauma patient
Coordinate rapid transition from chemical sedation to RSI
Apply MATC Head Injury protocol principles once control is achieved
Avoid physical restraint alone in the head-injured patient
discussionQuestions: Why does the MATC Behavioral protocol explicitly exclude head injury patients?
What made ketamine the right choice over haloperidol or droperidol here?
If ketamine provided the induction, why do you still need a paralytic?
What are the risks of NPA placement in this patient?
How would you document this encounter to show your clinical reasoning?
commonPitfalls: Treating the patient as a behavioral emergency and missing the head injury
Giving haloperidol or droperidol without addressing hypoxia
Failing to transition from chemical sedation to definitive airway
Placing an NPA in a patient with possible basilar skull fracture
Overdosing induction agents when IM ketamine already dissociated the patient
keyTakeaways: Agitation in trauma is hypoxia or head injury until proven otherwise
Ketamine IM is the bridge — RSI is the destination
Chemical sedation should always precede physical restraint in the head-injured patient
Don't stack sedatives unnecessarily; dissociation + paralytic is the formula
